serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Milanese di Pollo, including chicken, spinach, and tomatoes, is approximately 450 grams.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient data adapted from USDA FoodData Central for 'Chicken, breaded, fried' (FDC ID: 171077) and 'Spinach, raw' (FDC ID: 1109068) and 'Tomatoes, red, ripe, raw, year round average' (FDC ID: 170457). Values are weighted based on typical recipe proportions.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI estimated based on 'Chicken, breaded, fried' (analogous to chicken schnitzel, GI ~45) and considering the low GI contributions from spinach and tomatoes. The breading contributes to the carbohydrate load, but the high protein and fat content moderate the overall glycemic response.

hypertension.guidance: Monitor sodium intake from this dish, as 266.7mg/100g is borderline; consider reducing added salt and limiting high-sodium condiments or sides to manage blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate, high protein, and healthy fats
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low sugar and moderate carbohydrate content, combined with high protein and healthy fats from olive oil, contribute to a slow and steady glucose release, making this dish generally suitable for managing Type 2 Diabetes when portion sizes are controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content and cooking method
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: With saturated fat at 2.22g/100g and pan-frying in olive oil, this dish is a reasonable choice for cardiovascular health; however, individuals should be mindful of overall dietary saturated fat and cholesterol intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The presence of anti-inflammatory ingredients like spinach, olive oil, garlic, and cherry tomatoes makes this dish potentially beneficial for managing rheumatoid arthritis symptoms, though individual responses to nightshade vegetables like tomatoes should be observed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Milanese di Pollo is a classic Italian dish featuring a chicken breast that is pounded thin, breaded with flour, egg, and breadcrumbs, then pan-fried until golden and crispy. It is typically served with sautéed baby spinach and roasted cherry tomatoes. The dish offers a delightful contrast of textures, with the crispy exterior of the chicken and the tender, juicy interior. The accompanying vegetables provide freshness and a slight tang. Nutritionally, it is a good source of protein from the chicken, with moderate carbohydrates from the breading and vegetables, and healthy fats from the olive oil used in pan-frying. It is generally suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its balanced macronutrient profile and low sugar content, and can be part of a heart-healthy diet when prepared with minimal added salt.
common_user_queries: Nutritional value of Milanese di Pollo?

confidence_reason: Nutrient data is an adaptation from USDA FoodData Central for similar components, not an exact match for the combined dish. GI is an analogous estimate.
notes: Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are estimated based on typical composition of ingredients from USDA FoodData Central, as detailed combined dish data is not available.
